blocks.body: Types of Questions — Main question types in English.

blocks.heading: Yes / No Questions
blocks.body:
• Yes / No Questions take a yes or no answer: Do you like coffee?
  • An auxiliary verb comes before the subject: Does he know? Can they swim?
  • With to be, you simply invert the verb and the subject: Is she ready? Are you happy?
  • Structure: Auxiliary + Subject + Main Verb +...?

blocks.heading: Wh-Questions
blocks.body:
• Wh-Questions focus on question words such as who, what, where, when, why, how.
  • The key point is word order after the wh-word, the role of auxiliaries, and natural answer patterns.
  • Start with the most common words and then add more detailed models.

blocks.heading: Alternative Questions
blocks.body:
• Alternative Questions offer a choice with or: Do you prefer tea or coffee?
  • The structure follows a general question and adds options: Is it black or white?
  • The answer is one of the options: Tea, please.
  • They can also be longer: Would you like to go now or wait a bit?

blocks.heading: Topics in This Section
blocks.body:
TopicLevelDescription
Yes / No QuestionsA1Questions with yes / no answers
Wh-QuestionsA1–A2Questions with question words
Answers to QuestionsA1–A2Short and full answers
Alternative QuestionsA2Questions with a choice
Question TagsB1Question tags
Negative QuestionsB1Negative questions

blocks.heading: Question Tags
blocks.body:
• Question Tags are short questions at the end of a sentence: You like tea, don't you?
  • The rule is positive sentence + negative tag, or negative sentence + positive tag.
  • Examples: She is happy, isn't she? They don't know, do they?
  • Use them to confirm information or open a conversation.

blocks.heading: Study Tips
blocks.body:
🧠

Start with Yes / No Questions and Wh-Questions.

Answers to Questions and Alternative Questions extend the basic patterns.

Add Question Tags and Negative Questions after that.
